title: Go 1.26 Toolchain, Programs, Packages, and Evidence · Goroutine Forensics
summary: A small operations team needs a reproducible status tool, but its source, package boundary, formatter, build, runtime, and release identity are unclear. Learners isolate plausible failures, compare competing hypotheses, and record cause-level repair evidence. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a reproducible first-program and toolchain evidence record and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-…
objectives: Explain how source files, package declarations, imports, init order, and package main produce an executable program.
Choose and interpret go run, build, install, test, fmt, vet, doc, env, version, and help evidence for a bounded task.
Apply UTF-8 source, identifiers, keywords, tokens, semicolon insertion, comments, and formatting rules without relying on visual coincidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record · Debug Program Package Main
steps.instruction:
debug case — A debug status command is tested with Go 1.26.5 for linux/amd64 and the browser Yaegi subset. Predict package initialization, output, artifact identity, and which go fmt, test, vet, build, env, doc, or version evidence is still missing before release 4.

Inspect the Program Package Main trace for this debug case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?

Evidence target: Inspect the failure record · Debug Program Package Main.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for explain how source files, package declarations, imports, init order, and package main produce an executable program. For Inspect the failure record · Debug Program Package Main, keep Program Package Main connected to executable Go behavior evidence.
